title: Vital Signs: Measurement and Normal Adult Ranges
subtitle: The nurse measures vital signs accurately and knows the normal adult ranges — correct cuff size, an apical pulse for irregular rhythms, discreet respiratory counts, and reporting out-of-range values.

patient.name: Vital Signs
patient.admitDate: The nurse measures temperature, pulse, respirations, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation accurately.

nursesNote: The nurse measures vital signs accurately and recognizes normal adult ranges: heart rate 60-100 bpm, respirations 12-20/min, blood pressure below 120/80, oxygen saturation 95-100%, and temperature around 37°C (98.6°F), with pain assessed on a 0-10 scale. Blood pressure requires the correct cuff size (a cuff that is too small reads falsely high), the arm supported at heart level, and the patient rested; the nurse avoids the arm with an IV, a dialysis fistula, or on the mastectomy side. An apical pulse is counted for a full minute when the rhythm is irregular or before giving digoxin (which is held for a rate under 60). Respirations are counted discreetly so the patient does not alter their breathing. For orthostatic vital signs the nurse measures lying, sitting, and standing, where a systolic drop of 20 mmHg or a diastolic drop of 10 mmHg suggests orthostatic hypotension. Out-of-range values are reported.
providerOrders: Measure temperature, pulse, respirations, blood pressure, and SpO2
Use the correct cuff size and avoid arms with IV/fistula/mastectomy
Count an apical pulse for one minute when the rhythm is irregular
Hold digoxin and notify the provider for an apical rate under 60
Report any vital sign outside the normal range

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about vital signs?

opts.text: A normal adult heart rate is about 60-100 bpm
A normal adult respiratory rate is about 12-20/min
A normal oxygen saturation is about 95-100%
A normal adult resting blood pressure is around 200/110
Average normal body temperature is about 37°C (98.6°F)
An apical pulse is counted for a full minute when the rhythm is irregular
A blood pressure cuff that is too small gives a falsely high reading
Respirations are counted without telling the patient
Avoid taking BP in an arm with a dialysis fistula or on the mastectomy side
Caffeine and smoking just before a reading do not affect blood pressure
The apical pulse is checked before giving digoxin
A 20 mmHg systolic drop on standing suggests orthostatic hypotension

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order taking a manual blood pressure

opts.text: Have the patient rest, seated with the arm at heart level
Select the correct cuff size and apply it to the bare arm
Palpate the brachial artery and inflate the cuff
Slowly deflate while listening for Korotkoff sounds
Record the systolic and diastolic readings

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Within normal range or report it?

opts.text: Heart rate 78 bpm
Respirations 10/min
SpO2 97%
Blood pressure 190/104
Temperature 37°C (98.6°F)
Heart rate 44 bpm
Respirations 16/min
SpO2 86%

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: BP is 88/54 and the patient feels dizzy
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stay with the patient and ensure safety (lying down)
Recheck the blood pressure
Assess other vitals and symptoms
Notify the provider
Document and continue monitoring
Using the correct cuff size for the patient's arm
Taking BP in the arm with a dialysis fistula
Counting an apical pulse for a full minute when irregular
Telling the patient you are counting their respirations
Reporting a heart rate of 44 bpm
Ignoring an SpO2 of 86%
Holding digoxin for an apical rate under 60
Reusing an oversized cuff to save time
